narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and an LSAT score of 31, you've clearly put in significant effort to pursue law school, and we appreciate the determination that brought you this far. Your early academic performance placed you in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades and the 2nd decile for Year 3 at your school—below the typical student's standing—while your family's middle-income bracket aligns with many applicants, and your plan to attend full-time matches the norm for most students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 45.2%, tipping the scales just enough to result in the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting a bar failure, even more influential than other elements in your profile. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA was the most powerful positive influence, pulling the prediction toward success and outperforming the average of 3.21 seen in typical students. Your LSAT score, though below the dataset average of 36.70, also contributed favorably by nudging the odds slightly toward passing. Less impactful but still notable, your lower Year 1 decile and family income each added a small push toward the failure prediction, while your full-time status offered a minor boost. Compared to peers who typically pass, your early and mid-school deciles were notably weaker, which overshadowed strengths like your GPA despite your demographic background being common among applicants. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a whole, but rather how these academic markers stacked up against patterns in students who succeed on the bar. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, strengthening your high school decile performance through additional coursework or programs if possible, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your undergraduate achievements. Many students in similar situations improve these areas and gain admission elsewhere—your GPA is already a standout asset to build on. We're rooting for your next steps toward law school success.
